annotation_workflow.returns: this function has no return value
annotation_workflow.title: Register the virtual cell network modelling workflows

parameters.name: outputdir
parameters.text: NULL

parameters.name: name
parameters.text: NULL

parameters.name: localdb
parameters.text: NULL

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text: 'Sys.which'('diamond')

parameters.name: domain
parameters.text: 'c'('bacteria', 'plant', 'animal', 'fungi')

parameters.name: builds
parameters.text: 'c'('TRN_network', 'Metabolic_network')

modelling_cellgraph.description:
Executes the end-to-end workflow for constructing a virtual cell graph model
from an NCBI GenBank assembly source file. This function initializes the
environment, configures parameters, runs the annotation workflow, and
generates the corresponding project files and reports.
modelling_cellgraph.details:
This function acts as a high-level wrapper around the \code{WorkflowRender}
and \code{CellRender} engines. It sets up a temporary workspace (if not
provided), resolves dependencies (like the DIAMOND executable), and triggers
the specific annotation modules requested by the user.
modelling_cellgraph.examples:
#\dontrun{

# Run the workflow for a bacterial genome
modelling_cellgraph(
src = "path/to/bacteria_genome.gb",
outputdir = "path/to/output_workspace",
name = "E_coli_Model",
domain = "bacteria",
builds = c("TRN_network", "Metabolic_network"),
n_threads = 16
)

#}

parameters.name: src
parameters.text:
\code{character}. The file path to the NCBI GenBank assembly
source file.

parameters.name: outputdir
parameters.text:
\code{character}. The directory path for storing result
files and temporary workspace. Defaults to \code{NULL}, which will use
the parent directory of the \code{src} file.

parameters.name: name
parameters.text:
\code{character}. An optional custom name for the virtual cell
model. If \code{NULL}, a default name may be assigned by the rendering engine.

parameters.name: up_len
parameters.text:
\code{integer}. The length (in base pairs) of the upstream
region from the Transcription Start Site (TSS) to be extracted.
Defaults to 150.

parameters.name: localdb
parameters.text:
\code{character}. The file path to a local annotation
database. Defaults to \code{NULL}, which falls back to the internal
package data directory.

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text:
\code{character}. The file path to the DIAMOND executable
(used for fast sequence alignment). Defaults to the system path found
via \code{Sys.which("diamond")}.

parameters.name: domain
parameters.text:
\code{character}. The biological domain of the input organism.
Must be one of \code{"bacteria"}, \code{"plant"}, \code{"animal"}, or
\code{"fungi"}. Note: If multiple values are provided, only the first
element will be used.

parameters.name: builds
n_threads
parameters.text:
\code{integer}. The number of CPU threads to allocate
for parallel processing. Defaults to 32.

modelling_cellgraph.returns:
This function returns \code{invisible(NULL)}. However, as a side
effect, it generates a \code{release} subdirectory within the
\code{outputdir} containing:
\itemize{
\item \code{builder.gcproj}: The project configuration file.
\item \code{model.xml}: The core virtual cell graph model file.
}
Additionally, an HTML or PDF report summarizing the virtual cell
modeling results is typically generated in the output directory by
the workflow renderer.
modelling_cellgraph.title: Run the Virtual Cell Graph Modeling Workflow

make_diamond_hits.description:
Executes a DIAMOND blastp search workflow against multiple reference databases.
This function orchestrates the entire process: it configures the environment,
creates the necessary DIAMOND databases (if not already created), performs
the sequence alignment against EC numbers, subcellular locations, and
transcription factors, and manages the working directory state.
make_diamond_hits.details:
The function performs the following steps:
\enumerate{
\item Retrieves configuration for the DIAMOND path, database directory,
and thread count.
\item Sets the working directory to the specific workflow workspace.
\item Calls \code{make_diamond} to build binary databases from FASTA sources.
\item Runs DIAMOND blastp against three databases:
\itemize{
\item \strong{ec_number}: Enzyme Commission numbers.
\item \strong{subcellular}: Subcellular location predictions.
\item \strong{transcript_factor}: Transcription factors.
}
\item Restores the original working directory upon completion.
}

parameters.name: app
parameters.text:
The application object passed by the workflow engine.
Used for workflow context and configuration.

parameters.name: context
parameters.text: The execution context object provided by the workflow engine.

make_diamond_hits.returns:
This function is called for its side effects (creating .m8 output files).
It does not return a value explicitly to the R session, but generates
alignment result files in the workflow workspace.
make_diamond_hits.title: make_diamond_hits

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text: 'Sys.which'('diamond')

make_diamond.description:
Generates DIAMOND binary databases (`.dmnd`) from source FASTA files.
This utility function constructs three specific databases required for
the annotation workflow: Enzyme Commission (EC) numbers, Subcellular
locations, and Transcription Factors.

parameters.name: local_db
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path where
the source FASTA files are located.

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the path to the DIAMOND
executable. Defaults to \code{Sys.which("diamond")} if not provided.

make_diamond.returns:
Invisible \code{NULL}. This function is called for its side effect
of creating database files (e.g., \code{ec_number.dmnd}) in the current
working directory.
make_diamond.title: make_diamond

make_genbank_proj.description:
Loads GenBank source files specified in the application configuration,
initializes a new `project` object via `CellRender`, and saves the
resulting project state to disk. Additionally, it extracts and exports
essential FASTA files required for downstream bioinformatics analyses,
such as motif scanning and homology searches.
make_genbank_proj.details:
The function executes a sequential pipeline:
\enumerate{
\item \strong{Data Ingestion:} Reads GenBank files from the path
specified in \code{get_config("src")} using \code{load_genbanks()}.
\item \strong{Object Creation:} Wraps the raw GenBank data into a
standardized \code{project} object.
\item \strong{State Persistence:} Saves the core project object locally
to avoid redundant parsing in future workflow steps.
\item \strong{Sequence Export:** Formats and writes specific sequence
subsets (upstream loci and proteins) into standardized FASTA files
tailored for specific downstream tools.
}
make_genbank_proj.examples:
# Typically called internally by the CellRender workflow engine:
# make_genbank_proj(current_app, current_context)

parameters.name: app
parameters.text:
A CellRender workflow app object. Primarily used in this
function to resolve working file paths via \code{workfile()}.

parameters.name: context
parameters.text:
A CellRender workflow context object.
\emph{Note:} Currently unused in this specific function but required
by the standard workflow execution signature.

make_genbank_proj.title: Initialize a GenBank Project and Export Downstream Sequence Files

tfbs_motif_scanning.description:
Performs motif scanning on gene upstream sequences to identify
Transcription Factor Binding Sites (TFBS). This function is conditionally
executed and will only run if the \code{TRN_network} module is enabled in
the virtual cell build configuration.
tfbs_motif_scanning.details:
The function executes a specific workflow for regulatory network
construction:
\enumerate{
\item \strong{Input Retrieval}: Reads the TSS upstream region sequences
(generated from \code{make_genbank_proj}) as a FASTA file.
\item \strong{Database Query}: Constructs the path to the local MEME
motif database using the \code{localdb} and \code{domain} configurations.
\item \strong{Scanning}: Calls \code{GCModeller::scan_motifs} to scan
the upstream sequences against the motif database. The search uses
a strict identity cutoff of \code{0.9} and utilizes multithreading
based on the \code{n_threads} configuration.
\item \strong{Output}: Parses the motif seeds and their associated
Transcription Factors (TFs) and writes the result to \code{tfbs_motifs.csv}.
}
The output CSV file contains structured string representations of the
identified motifs and TFs. The parsed text formats are as follows:
\itemize{
\item \strong{Motif Seeds}: Formatted as
\code{"{motif.id} {motif.family} [{motif.name}]"}
(e.g., \code{"AsnC; MOTIF 557 AsnC [YwrC - Bacillales] Motif_1"}).
\item \strong{Transcription Factors}: Mapped via TF name and protein model,
formatted as \code{">{TF.name} {motif.id} Protein:{protein_model}"}
(e.g., \code{">jk0144 1269 Protein:144-jk0144"}).
}
tfbs_motif_scanning.examples:
# This function is typically executed internally by the GCModeller
# virtual cell framework based on the [@app] decorator, rather than
# called directly by the user.
# Example of manual configuration checks:
# check_build_module("TRN_network")

parameters.name: app
parameters.text:
The application environment or object, used to determine the
output working directory via \code{workfile()}.

parameters.name: context
parameters.text: The execution context passed by the GCModeller framework.

tfbs_motif_scanning.returns:
This function does not return an object to the R environment.
Its primary purpose is the side effect of generating the
\code{tfbs_motifs.csv} file in the application working directory.
tfbs_motif_scanning.title: Scan for Transcription Factor Binding Sites (TFBS) Motifs

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text: 'Sys.which'('diamond')

parameters.name: skip.blastp
parameters.text: FALSE

pangenome_analysis.description:
This function orchestrates a complete pan-genome analysis pipeline for a
set of microbial genomes. It extracts genomic data from GenBank files,
performs protein annotation via DIAMOND BLASTP, assigns orthology groups,
and calculates both Structural Variation (SV) and Presence/Absence Variation (PAV).
pangenome_analysis.details:
The pipeline proceeds through the following steps:
\itemize{
\item \strong{Extraction}: Extracts protein FASTA (.faa) and gene annotation
(.csv) files into a \code{source} subdirectory.
\item \strong{Annotation}: Runs DIAMOND BLASTP against an internal EC number
reference database using \code{\link{batch_diamond}}.
\item \strong{Orthology Assignment}: Reads BLASTP results (.m8), assigns terms
(keeping only the top best hit with >30\% identity, filtering unknowns), and
constructs orthology groups.
\item \strong{Pan-genome Construction}: Builds the pangenome context and
executes the core/accessory analysis using the defined ortholog sets.
\item \strong{Export}: Saves an interactive HTML report, an SV result table
(CSV), and a PAV result table (CSV) to \code{result_dir}.
}

parameters.name: src
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path containing the
input NCBI GenBank assembly files.

parameters.name: result_dir
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path where
all intermediate and final analysis results will be exported.

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the file path to the DIAMOND
executable. Defaults to the system PATH via \code{Sys.which("diamond")}.

parameters.name: n_threads
parameters.text:
An integer specifying the number of CPU threads to use for
the DIAMOND BLASTP search. Defaults to 32.

pangenome_analysis.returns:
Returns \code{invisible(NULL)}. All results are written directly to
the \code{result_dir} as side effects.
pangenome_analysis.title: Pan-genome Analysis of a Microbial Community

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text: 'Sys.which'('diamond')

batch_diamond.description:
A helper function that formats a local EC number reference database and
executes DIAMOND BLASTP in batch mode for all protein FASTA files in a
given source directory.
batch_diamond.details:
This function temporarily changes the working directory to \code{result_dir}
to generate the DIAMOND database (\code{ec_number.dmnd}) using an internal
package reference file (\code{ec_numbers.fasta}). It then iterates through
all \code{.faa} files in \code{source_dir} and executes a BLASTP search.
The original working directory is restored upon completion.

parameters.name: source_dir
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path containing
input protein FASTA files (\code{.faa}).

parameters.name: result_dir
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path where the
DIAMOND output files (\code{.m8} format) will be saved.

parameters.name: diamond
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the file path to the DIAMOND
executable. Defaults to \code{Sys.which("diamond")}.

parameters.name: n_threads
parameters.text:
An integer specifying the number of CPU threads to allocate
to DIAMOND. Defaults to 32.

batch_diamond.returns:
Returns \code{invisible(NULL)}. Output \code{.m8} files and the
DIAMOND database file are written to \code{result_dir}.
batch_diamond.title: Batch DIAMOND BLASTP Search Against Enzyme Database

parameters.name: tag.genbank.accid
parameters.text: FALSE

parameters.name: verbose
parameters.text: TRUE

extract_gbff.description:
This is a helper function that parses a GenBank assembly file to extract
genomic sequences, gene features, and the upstream regions relative to the
Transcription Start Sites (TSS). It is designed to prepare data for
downstream transcript regulation network analysis.
The function performs extensive file I/O operations. It extracts the raw
genomic FASTA, calculates the TSS upstream loci for each gene, cuts these
loci from the genome, and exports both the sequences and tabular context
files to the specified working directory.

parameters.name: src
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the file path to the input
GenBank (`.gb`, `.gbff`, or `.gbk`) file.

parameters.name: workdir
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path where
all output files will be saved. Defaults to `"./"` (current working directory).

parameters.name: upstream_size
parameters.text:
An integer specifying the length of the upstream region
(in base pairs) to extract relative to the gene TSS. Defaults to `150`.

parameters.name: tag_genbank_accid
parameters.text:
A logical value. If `TRUE`, the GenBank accession
ID is appended as the source tag in the FASTA headers of the extracted
upstream sequences. If `FALSE` (default), the original sequence headers
are used as the source tag.

parameters.name: verbose
parameters.text:
A logical value. If `TRUE` (default), progress messages
and the titles of the extracted upstream loci are printed to the console.

extract_gbff.returns:
Returns `invisible(NULL)`. This function is called primarily for
its side effects: generating and saving output files to the `workdir`.
extract_gbff.title: Extract Upstream Sequences and Annotations from a GenBank File

extract_genomes.description:
This function processes multiple GenBank files located in a source directory.
It extracts protein sequences and gene feature tables from each file and
exports them to a specified output directory. Specifically, it generates
FASTA files (`.faa`) for protein sequences and CSV files (`.csv`) for
gene annotation tables.
extract_genomes.details:
The function iterates through all matching GenBank files in the source
directory. For each file:
\itemize{
\item Reads the GenBank record.
\item Extracts the accession ID to name the output files.
\item Exports protein sequences in FASTA format, using the gene locus tag
as the sequence header.
\item Exports gene features (ORFs) as a tabular CSV file.
}
extract_genomes.examples:
#\dontrun{

# Define source and output directories
source_path <- "path/to/genbank_files"
output_path <- "path/to/output_data"
# Run the extraction
extract_genomes(src = source_path, outputdir = output_path)

#}

parameters.name: src
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path containing the
GenBank source files. The function scans for files with extensions
`.gb`, `.gbk`, or `.gbff`.

parameters.name: outputdir
parameters.text:
A character string specifying the directory path where the
extracted data files will be saved. If the directory does not exist, it
should be created beforehand (or the function may fail depending on
implementation).

extract_genomes.returns:
Invisible `NULL`. This function is called for its side effects
(writing files to disk) and does not return a value to the console.
extract_genomes.title: Extract Protein Sequences and Gene Features from Batch GenBank Files
